name: Toyol
description: The Toyol is a mischievous and malevolent spirit in Malay folklore, often described as a small, infant-like being created through black magic. It is typically made from a stillborn fetus or a corpse of a young child, brought to life by a bomoh (Malay shaman) or a witch doctor. The Toyol is bound to its master and used for various purposes, most commonly theft and causing harm to enemies. While not inherently powerful physically, its small size and ability to move unseen make it a formidable nuisance. It requires constant attention and tasks to keep it occupied, otherwise it will turn on its master. Feeding it is also crucial, often with milk or blood, to maintain its loyalty and power. Stories of Toyol often serve as cautionary tales against practicing black magic and the dangers of tampering with the natural order.
subtitle: Infant spirit of mischief and theft
appearance: Toyols are typically depicted as small, naked, and infantile beings, resembling a baby or a young child. Their skin is often described as pale, greyish, or even greenish, reflecting their undead nature. They may have disproportionately large heads or eyes, and their features are often distorted or unsettling. Some accounts mention them having sharp teeth or claws, although this is less common. They are generally portrayed as being unkempt and dirty, adding to their frightening appearance. Due to their magical origins, their form is not fixed and can vary slightly depending on the specific ritual used to create them.
habitat: Toyols do not have a specific natural habitat. They are bound to their master and typically reside in the master's home or the location where they are instructed to carry out their tasks. They can be found in houses, gardens, or even hidden within objects.
behavior: Toyols are inherently mischievous and driven by a childlike curiosity. They are obedient to their master but require constant supervision and tasks to keep them occupied. If left idle, they will engage in random acts of mischief, often escalating into more destructive or harmful behavior. They are easily distracted by toys or shiny objects. Their loyalty is maintained through regular feeding, usually with milk or blood. They are also known to be easily frightened by religious symbols or prayers.

keyMyths.title: The Toyol and the Stolen Gold
keyMyths.description: A wealthy merchant used a Toyol to steal gold from his neighbors. However, he neglected to feed the Toyol properly, and it eventually turned on him, stealing all his own gold and leaving him penniless.

keyMyths.title: Protecting Your Home from a Toyol
keyMyths.description: Villagers often place needles or broken glass under their doorways to deter Toyols from entering their homes. This is because the Toyol, being childlike, is easily injured and will avoid such obstacles.

sources.author: Rosemary Ellen Guiley
sources.work: The Encyclopedia of Demons and Demonology
sources.description: Provides a general overview of demons and related creatures, including brief mentions of similar Southeast Asian entities.
sources.text: Provides a general overview of demons and related creatures, including brief mentions of similar Southeast Asian entities.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Numerous collections of Malay folktales and myths often contain stories about Toyols and their interactions with humans.
sources.text: Numerous collections of Malay folktales and myths often contain stories about Toyols and their interactions with humans.
